items.title: Emergent Ontology (EO)
items.excerpt: Emergent Ontology (/ɪˈmɜːrdʒənt ɒnˈtɒlədʒi/; abbreviated EO /ˌiːˈoʊ/) is a meta-theoretical framework built on a single claim: whatever can be expressed about transformation has tripartite structure. items.title: Time as Emergent Dimension
items.excerpt: Emergent Ontology (EO) proposes that the three axes of its capacity ground — Identity (Mode), Space (Domain), and Time (Object) — are not three independently given dimensions. items.title: Ground / Figure / Pattern

items.title: Degrees of Freedom
items.excerpt: Degrees of freedom in Emergent Ontology (E.O.) are successive liberations in what a system can know, represent, and act on. items.title: Decomposability

items.title: The Nine Operators

items.title: The Triadic Minimum
items.excerpt: The Triadic Minimum is a foundational principle in Emergent Ontology (EO). It asserts that three elements constitute the minimal structure required for stable differentiation, relation, and transformation. items.title: The Three Arrows
items.excerpt: The arrow of time is not one arrow. It is three arrows from three sources, each living on a different axis of EO's capacity ground.
